sections.title: What the present perfect does (core logic)
sections.content:
The present perfect links a **past action to NOW**. The form is **have/has + past participle** — *I have worked, she has gone, they have seen*. The action started or happened in the past, but the speaker is pointing at its **present relevance**: a current result, a life experience that still counts, or a duration that reaches up to now.

**Why it matters (roots-first)**: This is one of the core tenses of the **Verb Phrase** root (#2 in the Tree Model). English has two grammatical aspects — **perfect** (action with present relevance) and **continuous** (action in progress) — and present perfect is the textbook example of the perfect aspect. Czech has no direct equivalent, so this is where Czech speakers most often slip.

**Where it shows up**: news (*Scientists have discovered…*), CVs and interviews (*I have worked at…*), small talk about life experience (*Have you ever been to Japan?*), and professional emails (*We have received your order*). Getting it right is one of the biggest upgrades from intermediate to confident English.

sections.title: Forming the present perfect — simple vs continuous
sections.content:
Within the present perfect there are **two forms**. Both link past to now, but they emphasise different things.

- **Simple**: have/has + past participle → *I have worked, she has gone*
- **Continuous**: have/has been + -ing → *I have been working, she has been going*

**Contractions** are the norm in speech: *I've, you've, we've, they've, he's, she's, it's*. (Note: *she's been* = *she has been*, not *she is been*.)

**Negatives**: haven't / hasn't + past participle (or *been + -ing*).
**Questions**: Have/Has + subject + past participle (or *been + -ing*)?

**Why it matters**: These are the two physical shapes you have to recognise and produce. The next sections cover the meaning choice between them.

diagram.title: Present perfect — forms
rows: I **have been working** / she **has been working**
I've been working / she's been working
I **haven't been working** / she **hasn't been working**
**Have** you been working? / **Has** she been working?

sections.title: Three uses of the SIMPLE
sections.content:
The present perfect simple has **three main uses** — learn them as three jobs the same tense does.

1. **Life experience** (at some unspecified point before now): *I have visited Paris. Have you ever eaten sushi? She has never flown.* — the time isn't important; the experience is.
2. **Recent action with a present result**: *I've broken my arm* (it's in a cast now). *They've arrived* (so they're here). *She's lost her keys* (she can't get in).
3. **Duration up to now — with stative verbs**: *I have known him for ten years. She has had that job since 2019. We have lived here for ages.* For action verbs, duration usually takes continuous (see next section).

**Common time markers** for present perfect simple: **ever, never, already, yet, just, so far, recently** — plus **since** and **for** with stative verbs.

**Why it matters**: These three uses cover most of the present perfect you'll see in textbooks, exams, and conversation. If you can name which of the three you mean before you speak, your tense choice becomes automatic.

sections.title: CONTINUOUS — emphasis on activity and duration
sections.content:
The present perfect continuous emphasises the **activity itself** — its duration, or its recent visibility — rather than the result.

- **Duration of an action**: *I've been studying English for three years. They've been living in Prague since 2019.* The focus is on the studying / living as an ongoing activity.
- **Recently stopped, with visible evidence now**: *Have you been crying?* (your eyes are red). *Why are you out of breath? — I've been running.* You can see the activity in the result.
- **Temporary or repeated recent activity**: *We've been looking for a flat for months. He's been working from home lately.*

**Contrast with simple**: *I've read three books this month* (result: three completed) vs *I've been reading all morning* (activity, no finished count).

**Important limit**: **stative verbs** (know, believe, own, belong, understand, like, want, need) do NOT take the continuous form. Wrong: *I've been knowing him for years.* Right: *I've known him for years.*

**Why it matters**: This is the form that most directly answers *"what have you been doing?"* — the natural register for talking about ongoing activity and effort at work, in lessons, and in everyday catch-ups.

sections.title: Simple vs continuous — making the choice
sections.content:
Both forms link past to now. The choice is about **what the speaker is pointing at**.

- **Simple** = the **result**, the **completion**, the **count**, the **fact**. Look at the outcome.
- **Continuous** = the **activity**, the **duration emphasis**, the **recent visible action**. Look at the process.

**Same situation, different frames**:
- *I've read three books this year.* (count → simple)
- *I've been reading all morning.* (activity → continuous)
- *She's painted the kitchen.* (it's done → simple)
- *She's been painting the kitchen.* (that's why she's covered in paint → continuous)

**Why it matters**: This is the single most-tested distinction at B1–B2 — Cambridge gap-fills, FCE Use of English, interview English. Once you can name *result vs activity*, the choice stops being guesswork.

diagram.title: Simple vs continuous — the choice
rows: **Activity in progress** (I've been writing the report.)
**How long — action verbs** (She's been sending emails all day.)
**Life experience** (Have you ever been to Japan?)
**Temporary / repeated** (We've been looking for a flat for months.)

sections.title: Since & for — both forms
sections.content:
**Since** and **for** are the two duration words that go with present perfect — simple or continuous.

- **since** + **starting point**: *since 2019, since Monday, since I was a child, since lunchtime*
- **for** + **length of time**: *for three years, for ten minutes, for ages, for as long as I can remember*

Both work with **simple** (usually stative verbs) and **continuous** (action verbs):
- *I've **known** her **since** primary school.* (stative → simple)
- *I've **been studying** Czech **for** three years.* (action → continuous)
- *They've **lived** / **been living** here **since** 2019.* (either is fine for *live*)

**Czech-speaker note — major error zone**: Czech uses the **present tense** for situations that started in the past and are still going on (*Bydlím tady tři roky* = literally *I live here three years*). In English this MUST be present perfect: *I have lived here for three years* or *I have been living here for three years* — NEVER *I live here for three years*. This is the single most common Czech-speaker mistake with this tense.

**Why it matters**: *Since* and *for* appear in almost every CV, interview, and getting-to-know-you conversation. Fixing this pattern alone removes a constant low-level error.

sections.title: Common Czech-speaker errors & where to go next
sections.content:
**The four error patterns to watch for**:

- **Present simple instead of present perfect** for ongoing situations: ✗ *I live here for three years.* → ✓ *I have lived here for three years.* (Czech *bydlím* = English *have lived*.)
- **Stative verb in continuous**: ✗ *I have been knowing him for years.* → ✓ *I have known him for years.* (No continuous with *know, believe, own, want, need*.)
- **Simple where continuous fits better** (activity focus): ✗ *Why are you tired? I've worked all day.* → more natural *I've been working all day.* (visible activity)
- **Continuous where simple fits better** (count/result focus): ✗ *I've been reading three books this month.* → ✓ *I've read three books this month.* (count = simple)

**Don't confuse with past simple** — that's a different topic. The contrast between **present perfect** (connected to now) and **past simple** (finished, separate) — including time markers like *yesterday, ago, last week* vs *ever, never, just, since, for* — is covered in the dedicated **Past Simple vs Present Perfect** topic. Go there for the past-vs-perfect choice; this topic stays inside the perfect aspect itself.

**Why it matters**: Once you've cleaned up these four patterns, the present perfect becomes a tool you reach for confidently — not a guess.

questions.question: We want to say how long something has been happening. Which form do we use?
questions.explanation: How long = we use present perfect continuous.

questions.question: Which sentence is correct? (Stative verb)

options.a: I have been knowing him for years.
options.c: I have known him for years.
options.d: I am knowing him for years.

questions.explanation: 'Know' is stative. We use present perfect simple.

questions.question: We want to say how many things are done. Which form do we use?
questions.explanation: How many completed = present perfect simple.

questions.question: Someone's hands are dirty. They did an activity. Which is more natural?

options.d: I have been repairing the bike.

questions.explanation: What you see now = present perfect continuous.

mc.title: Practice: Present Perfect Simple vs Continuous

questions.question: We want to say how long something has been happening. Which form do we use?
questions.explanation: How long = we use present perfect continuous.

questions.question: Which sentence is correct? (Stative verb)

options.a: I have been knowing him for years.
options.c: I have known him for years.
options.d: I am knowing him for years.

questions.explanation: 'Know' is stative. We use present perfect simple.

questions.question: We want to say how many things are done. Which form do we use?
questions.explanation: How many completed = present perfect simple.

questions.question: Someone's hands are dirty. They did an activity. Which is more natural?

options.d: I have been repairing the bike.

questions.explanation: What you see now = present perfect continuous.

questions.question: Which sentence is correct? (Stative verb 'have')

options.a: She has been having that car since 2020.
options.b: She has had that car since 2020.
options.c: She is having that car since 2020.

questions.explanation: 'Have' (possession) is stative; we use the present perfect simple.

questions.question: 'I've read three books this month.' – Why do we use the simple form?

options.b: We focus on how many completed.
options.c: We focus on visible effect now.
options.d: We focus on activity in progress.

questions.explanation: How many completed → present perfect simple.

questions.question: 'They've been looking for a flat for months.' – Why do we use the continuous form?

options.a: We focus on the number of flats.
options.c: We focus on a stative verb.
options.d: We focus on a single completed action.

questions.explanation: How long = present perfect continuous.

questions.question: Which is wrong? (Stative verb)

options.a: I have known him for years.
options.b: I have been knowing him for years.
options.c: She has had that job since 2019.
options.d: We have believed in it for a long time.

questions.explanation: 'Know' is stative; we do not use the continuous form.

questions.question: We want to say how long something has been happening. Which form do we use?
questions.explanation: How long = we use present perfect continuous.

questions.question: We want to say how many things are done. Which form do we use?
questions.explanation: How many completed = present perfect simple.

questions.question: I'm sorry I'm late. I ____ (wait) for the train for almost an hour.
answers: have been waiting
questions.explanation: How long = present perfect continuous.

questions.question: She ____ (read) five books so far this summer.
answers: has read
questions.explanation: How many completed = present perfect simple.

questions.question: Your hair is wet. ____ you ____ (run) in the rain?
answers: have you been running
questions.explanation: What you see now = present perfect continuous.

questions.question: I ____ (know) him since we started high school.
answers: have known
questions.explanation: 'Know' is stative; we use the present perfect simple.

questions.question: They ____ (not sleep) well lately because of the noisy neighbours.
answers: have not been sleeping
haven't been sleeping
questions.explanation: Something that is still going on lately = present perfect continuous.

questions.question: He ____ (send) ten job applications this week.
answers: has sent
questions.explanation: How many completed → present perfect simple.

questions.question: She ____ (have) that car since 2020.
answers: has had
questions.explanation: 'Have' (possession) is stative. Present perfect simple.

questions.question: Wrong: 'I have been loving this new series since the first episode.' Correct the sentence.
answers: I have loved this new series since the first episode.
I've loved this new series since the first episode.
I have loved this new series since the first episode
I've loved this new series since the first episode
questions.explanation: 'Love' is stative; we use the present perfect simple.

questions.question: Wrong: 'I have been having this phone for two years.' Correct the sentence.
answers: I have had this phone for two years.
I've had this phone for two years.
I have had this phone for two years
I've had this phone for two years
questions.explanation: 'Have' (possession) is stative. Present perfect simple.

questions.question: Wrong: 'She has been owning that shop since 2019.' Correct the sentence.
answers: She has owned that shop since 2019.
She's owned that shop since 2019.
She has owned that shop since 2019
She's owned that shop since 2019
questions.explanation: 'Own' is stative; we use the present perfect simple.

questions.question: Wrong: 'We have been believing in recycling for years.' Correct the sentence.
answers: We have believed in recycling for years.
We've believed in recycling for years.
We have believed in recycling for years
We've believed in recycling for years
questions.explanation: 'Believe' is stative; present perfect simple.

makesentence.title: Practice: Making sentences (make a full sentence from the cues)

answers: I have been waiting for the bus for 20 minutes.
I've been waiting for the bus for 20 minutes.
questions.explanation: How long = present perfect continuous.

questions.prompt: she / read / three books / this month
answers: She has read three books this month.
She's read three books this month.
questions.explanation: How many completed → present perfect simple.

questions.prompt: you / know / your best friend / since primary school
answers: I have known my best friend since primary school.
I've known my best friend since primary school.
questions.explanation: Stative verb 'know' → present perfect simple.

answers: They have been living in London for five years.
They've been living in London for five years.
They have lived in London for five years.
They've lived in London for five years.
questions.explanation: Both forms are OK here with 'live'.

answers: He has just finished his homework.
He's just finished his homework.
questions.explanation: Result = present perfect simple.

questions.prompt: you / study / all evening (you are tired now)
answers: I have been studying all evening.
I've been studying all evening.
questions.explanation: How long and what you see now (tired) = present perfect continuous.

makequestion.title: Practice: Making questions (make a question from the cues)

answers: How long have they been learning English?
How long have they been learning English
questions.explanation: How long = present perfect continuous question.

questions.prompt: she / read / books / this year / how many
answers: How many books has she read this year?
How many books has she read this year
questions.explanation: How many done = present perfect simple question.

questions.prompt: he / out of breath – suggest: exercise
answers: Have you been exercising?
Have you been exercising
questions.explanation: What you see now = present perfect continuous question.

answers: Have they ever been to Japan?
Have they ever been to Japan
questions.explanation: Experience = present perfect simple question.

answers: How long has she had her car?
How long has she had her car
questions.explanation: Stative 'have' (possession) = present perfect simple question.

questions.prompt: they / do / all morning / what (they look tired)
answers: What have they been doing all morning?
What have they been doing all morning
questions.explanation: How long and what you see = present perfect continuous question.
